entry.title: 5 Things You Need to Know Before Starting with SAP S/4HANA
entry.content:
SAP S/4HANA is one of the most in-demand skills in today’s SAP ecosystem, but it can feel overwhelming to understand what it actually is and why it matters. 

Is it just a database upgrade? A completely new system? And why are so many companies rushing to adopt it before 2027? 

In this guide, alongside João Simões , Principal Software Architect at SAP Portugal, we break down SAP S/4HANA in simple terms: what it is, how it differs from SAP ECC, why companies are migrating, and the key things you need to know before getting started. 

What is SAP S/4HANA? 

 is a modern Enterprise Resource Planning (ERP) suite designed to help organizations run their core business processes in real time. It is built on the SAP HANA (in-memory database), which allows companies to process and analyze large volumes of data instantly. As a result, this ERP delivers significantly faster performance than traditional systems. 

Since it is designed to centralize business processes, SAP S/4HANA integrates major business functions such as finance, supply chain, manufacturing, HR, and sales into a single platform, providing users with a more intuitive and mobile-friendly experience through Versions Available 

  • Cloud (S/4HANA Cloud): SaaS offering with regular updates
  • On-Premise (S/4HANA): Deployed on company servers with more customization options 

Why Are Companies Moving to SAP S/4HANA? 

Transitioning to SAP S/4HANA has become critical because January 1st 2027, marks the end of mainstream support for SAP ERP (Business Suite). After this date, organizations running SAP ECC will no longer receive new security patches, bug fixes, compliance & audit risks, or technical support from SAP.  

Continuing to operate with unsupported systems can expose companies to increasing cybersecurity risks, compliance risks, and higher operational costs. For many organizations, this creates a limited migration window of the next 2-3 years to plan and execute their transition to SAP S/4HANA.  

However, migrating to S/4HANA is not only driven by the deadline, but also by 3 key factors:

  • Financial risks → Running unsupported systems can lead to cybersecurity vulnerabilities, regulatory risks (eg. GDPR), and higher costs for maintaining legacy infrastructure. 
  • Technical obsolescence → Legacy ERP systems can’t integrate with Industry 4.0 tech such as #cloud, #AI, and #IoT, making business processes increasingly brittle, without mentioning that staff expertise of old systems becomes unavailable.  
  • Competitive disadvantage → Modern businesses require real-time insights, agility, Cloud flexibility, and a digital-first UX, capabilities that SAP S/4HANA is designed to support. 

SAP ECC vs SAP S/4HANA: Key Differences Explained 

Feature 

SAP ECC 

SAP S/4HANA 

Database 

Oracle, SQL Server, DB2 

SAP HANA only 

Architecture 

Three-tier (Presentation, Application, Database) 

Simplified, HANA-optimized 

In-Memory Processing 

Limited 

Built-in & native 

Performance 

Traditional disk-based; slower 

Real-time processing; 10x faster 

Reporting & Analytics 

Limited analytics capabilities 

Instant analytics & reporting 

Data Model 

Complex structures; many aggregation tables 

Simplified tables; reduced redundancy 

Storage Requirements 

High 

Reduced 

User Interface 

Older, traditional interface 

Modern Fiori interface 

Mobility 

Limited mobile support 

Mobile-friendly 

AI/ML Capabilities 

Not available 

Integrated AI/ML 

IoT Integration 

Limited 

Full IoT support 

Cloud Ready 

Not optimized for cloud 

Cloud-native 

Upfront Cost 

Lower 

Higher 

Operational Cost 

High maintenance overhead 

Lower operational costs 

Maintenance 

Complex, time-consuming 

Reduced maintenance 

End of Support 

2027 (mainstream support) 

Actively supported & developed 

User Experience 

Functional but dated 

Intuitive & modern 

What does SAP S/4HANA actually does? (Core Capabilities) 

As mentioned before, it enables organizations make faster decisions and operate more efficiently.  Core capabilities include: 

  1. Real-Time Analytics & Reporting
  2. AI & Machine Learning
    • Demand Forecasting: AI predicts product demand to optimize inventory
    • Fraud Detection: Machine learning flags suspicious transactions automatically
    • Equipment Maintenance: Predictive models warn of failures before they happen
    • Customer Analytics: AI identifies high-value customers and churn risks
  3. User Experience via SAP Fiori
  4. Business Process Automation
  5. Simplified Data Model & Performance
    • Faster reporting: Reports that took 30 minutes now take 2 seconds
    • Lower costs: Reduced storage and server infrastructure
    • Better decisions: Real-time data vs. stale data
    • Easier maintenance: Fewer tables to manage
  6. Financial Management & Planning
  7. Supply Chain Optimization
    • Reduced stockouts: 15–20% inventory reduction
    • Faster delivery: Optimized routes & automated processes
    • Cost savings: 10–15% logistics cost reduction
    • Risk mitigation: Early warning of supply disruptions
  8. Sales & Customer Experience
  9. Integration with External Systems
  10. Compliance & Risk Management
  11. Cloud & Scalability
  12. Enhanced Planning & Forecasting

To illustrate the impact, consider a bakery that supplies pastries to cafés and supermarkets. The order-to-cash process can change significantly when moving from SAP ECC to SAP S/4HANA:  

Phase 

Before (ECC) 

After (S/4HANA) 

Order Received 

Manual entry, 2 hours 

Auto-captured, instant 

Inventory Check 

Query & wait, 30 mins 

Real-time visibility, 1 second 

Pricing 

Manual calculation, errors 

Dynamic AI pricing, instant 

Quote 

Created next day 

Sent in 5 minutes 

Production Plan 

Scheduled weekly 

Optimized hourly by AI 

Shipment 

Routed manually 

AI optimizes for cost/speed 

Invoice 

Generated, sent week later 

Auto-generated at shipment 

Payment 

30-day terms average 

Accelerated to 15 days 

How To Pick The Right SAP S/4HANA Deployment Model? 

Organizations can deploy SAP S/4HANA in several ways depending on their business needs, level of customization, and IT strategy. The three main deployment options are:  

Dimension  

Public Cloud (GROW) 

Private Cloud (RISE) 

On-Premise 

Innovation Cycle 

Semi-annual 

FPS 6 months; Major releases 2 years 

 

TCO 

Lowest 

Lower 

Higher 

License 

Subscription 

Subscription 

Software License 

IT Infra 

SAP, shared 

Dedicated 

Customer Managed 

Customizations 

Within Standards 

Within Standards 

Open-to-mods 

Governance 

SAP-led 

Customer-influenced 

Customer-led 

How Much Does SAP S/4HANA Cost? 

SAP S/4HANA pricing can vary significantly depending on the organization’s size, system complexity, and deployment model. Instead of a fixed price, the total cost of ownership (TCO) depends on several factors, such as:  

  • Number of users (biggest factor) 
  • Deployment type (cloud vs on-premise) 
  • Modules required (Finance, Supply Chain, HR, etc.) 
  • Industry-specific solutions 
  • Implementation complexity
  • Company size & revenue  

Cloud vs On-Premise Comparison 

Aspect 

Cloud 

On-Premise 

Initial licensing cost 

Lower 

Higher 

Infrastructure cost 

Included 

Higher 

Implementation time 

Faster 

Slower 

Total Cost of Ownership (3-5 years) 

Often lower 

Often higher 

Is SAP S/4HANA Difficult To Learn? 

No. Like any enterprise system, SAP S/4HANA comes with a learning curve but is more than doable! Depending on the learning track, you can get certified between 2 weeks up to 2 months.  

For those interested in building their skills in this area, the SAP Young Professionals program offers a fully sponsored, world class training experience, where you will earn SAP Certifications, gain hands-on experience with SAP solutions, and get introduced into the SAP Ecosystem to work with Partners, Clients, and even SAP. Check out our current openings.  

If none of the openings are suitable for your region at the moment, we invite you to explore all courses on SAP Learning, where you can get certifications with self-paced e-learning courses.

entry.title: Remittance Advice in SAP: What I Learned While Fixing Vendor Payment Issues
entry.content:
Why Remittance Advice Became Critical in My Project

In one of my projects, we started receiving frequent vendor complaints even though payments were being processed on time through the Automatic Payment Program (F110). Vendors were reaching out saying invoices were still showing as open on their side. After analyzing the issue, we realized the problem was not with the payment itself, but with the remittance advice. The information being sent to vendors was not clear enough for them to reconcile payments correctly.

That’s when I understood that remittance advice is not just a payment notification it plays a key role in post-payment reconciliation.

Unclear remittance advice started creating several operational challenges. Vendors were often unable to clearly identify which invoices had been paid, especially in cases where deductions like withholding tax or early payment discounts were involved. This led to payments being incorrectly applied to older or unrelated invoices.
As a result, our team began receiving a high volume of vendor queries, spending significant time investigating and clarifying payment details. During month-end, we also noticed discrepancies between vendor statements and our sub-ledger, which made reconciliation more difficult. Overall, these issues not only increased internal effort but also impacted vendor trust and communication.

To address this, I got involved in improving the remittance advice as part of an enhancement activity. While working with the F110 payment program, I first spent some time understanding how the remittance advice was being generated and where the gaps were.
I noticed that the standard output was not providing enough clarity at the invoice level, especially when deductions like tax or discounts were involved. So, I worked closely with our ABAP team to enhance the DMEE structure and include more detailed invoice-level information.
Along with that, we updated the Adobe Form layout used for remittance advice output to make the communication clearer for vendors specifically around invoice numbers, payment amounts, and deductions.
We also reviewed the vendor master data to ensure the correct communication method, such as EDI, was properly maintained so that the remittance advice consistently reached vendors in the expected format.

During this process, we also came across a few challenges:

One of the main challenges we faced was ensuring all deductions, such as tax and discounts, were clearly reflected in the remittance advice, as the standard output lacked sufficient detail. We also had issues with formatting and communication settings, which sometimes caused confusion for vendors. Overall, the challenge was making the remittance both clear and consistently delivered.

After implementing these changes, we observed:

  • Vendor queries reduced significantly
  • Vendors were able to automatically clear invoices on their side
  • Month-end reconciliation became smoother
  • Our team spent less time on manual clarification and follow-ups

This clearly showed how critical remittance advice is beyond just sending payment confirmation.

Conclusion

From my experience, remittance advice plays a crucial role in maintaining transparency between Accounts Payable and vendors. Even when payments are accurate, poor communication can create confusion and additional workload.
Getting the remittance process right, especially during implementation or enhancement, can prevent many post-go-live issues and improve overall efficiency.

entry.title: From User Assistance Developer to Agent Builder: My First Step into AI Workflows
entry.content:
I've spent years as a User Assistance Developer creating help documentation, improving clarity, and thinking from a user's perspective. Recently, I found myself asking a simple question, "Can I use AI to review documentation the way I do?"

I didn't want to become a developer. I just wanted to make my work smarter. 

That's when I started experimenting with AI tools like Claude Code. My goal was simple - build something that could read help documentation and suggest improvements from a customer's point of view.

At first, it sounded straightforward, give our help portal URL, get feedback. But I quickly learned that modern websites don't always expose their content directly. Many pages load content using JavaScript, which meant I had to use a browser automation tool like Puppeteer just to see what a user sees.

What started as a small idea turned into a structured workflow.

I created an agent that:

- Reads documentation pages
- Extracts meaningful content
- Reviews it from both technical and non-technical perspectives
- Identifies what is missing, confusing, or poorly structured
- Suggests specific improvements

But the real breakthrough came when it was made efficient.

Instead of reading the website every time (which was slow), I designed it to:

- Read once and store the content
- Reuse that content for future analysis
- Only refresh when needed

This made the process fast, repeatable, and practical.

Where Claude became my developer partner

What made this journey possible was how I used Claude, not just as a chatbot, but as a fellow developer.

I didn't start with code. I started with questions.

  • How do I extract content from a webpage that loads dynamically?
  • Why isn’t this script working?
  • Can you simplify this setup for me?

Claude didn't just give answers, it helped me think through the problem step by step.

When I got stuck installing tools or setting up the environment, it guided me through it patiently. When my code didn’t work, it debugged with me. When things felt too complex, it simplified them. At one point, I realized I wasn't just asking for solutions, I was iterating with it, like I would with a teammate.

What actually surprised me most wasn't the technology; it was the shift in how I think about my role. I'm no longer just writing documentation. I'm designing systems that improve documentation. And I did this without writing complex code or becoming a full-time developer.

If you are a writer or someone from a non-technical background, this is my takeaway:

You don't need to “become a developer” to work with AI.
You just need to understand your problem deeply and let AI help you build around it.

 

Happy vibe-coding!

entry.title: What's New in SAP Asset Performance Management 2603
entry.content:
What do you do when a released assessment no longer reflects reality? That's the main topic of this month's release in SAP Asset Performance Management. We also have a very useful enhancement for directly accessing help within your workflow. With SAP Companion, you can now access contextual guidance, documentation, and explanations exactly where you need them — without leaving the application. Let's get into the details.

Reassess the risk and criticality of technical objects

The Challenge

In asset-intensive industries, change is constant.
Asset conditions evolve, maintenance strategies are refined, and new insights emerge over time.

Risk and Criticality Assessments are designed to reflect these realities — but until now, once an assessment was released, it could no longer be revisited.

This created a gap:

  • Assessments could become outdated as validity periods expired
  • Changes in templates or strategies were difficult to reflect
  • Users often had to recreate assessments from scratch
  • There was limited visibility into how risk evolved over time

The Solution

With the introduction of Reassessment with Versioning, you can now revise released assessments while maintaining full history.

Instead of replacing an assessment, the system creates a new version in Created status, while the currently released version remains active. This allows you to safely update assessments without disrupting ongoing operations.

During reassessment, the system supports you by:

  • Evaluating assigned technical objects and highlighting conflicts
  • Identifying objects that cannot be carried forward, such as those already part of ongoing assessments, inactive, or marked for deletion
  • Allowing you to decide which objects to carry forward
  • Highlighting obsolete templates and ensuring valid versions are used
  • Preserving role assignments for consistency

Reassessment of Risk and Criticality Assessment and Versioning

The Outcome

Reassessment with versioning brings flexibility and control to the assessment process.

You can now:

  • Keep assessments aligned with changing conditions and strategies
  • Maintain continuity without recreating assessments
  • Compare versions to track how risk evolves over time
  • Validate decisions and ensure consistency
  • Provide full traceability and auditability

Once the new version is released, the previous version is automatically marked as obsolete — ensuring a clear and structured lifecycle. For more information see the SAP Help Portal.

Overall, this helps ensure that your risk and criticality assessments remain accurate, transparent, and aligned with operational reality.

Help Where You Need It - Improvements to SAP Companion

You’re in the middle of a task, something isn’t clear, and suddenly you’re forced to stop — search documentation, open another tab, or dig through support material.

That interruption breaks your flow.

With the contextual help panel in the Fiori Launchpad, guidance is available directly within the application.

Users can access:

  • Contextual explanations
  • Documentation links
  • Technical component details

All without leaving their current workspace. For more information, see @Sivda's blog Get Help Where You Need it: SAP Companion in SAP Asset Performance Management and watch the video:

Click thumbnail to watch the video "Get Help Where You Need It - SAP Companion"

Stay Informed and Get Involved

  • You can explore our latest blog posts here, including topics such as Embedded IoT by @florian_weigold, Industrial Systems by @SonamKinger, and the extensibility blog by @archigarg.
  • Find out what's up and coming in the SAP Road Map Explorer.
  • For an exhaustive list of all new features, visit the SAP Help Portal - What's New in SAP Asset Performance Management.
  • Send us your improvement requests. Submit or vote for the improvement you would like to see in the SAP Continuous Influence session.
  • Check the latest update of the APM link collection. 

See you for the 2604 update!

entry.title: How Business Information Extraction in SAP Sales Cloud V2 Accelerates Sales Response and Conversion
entry.content:
What you will get from this blog

In this blog, you’ll understand how Business Information Extraction (BIE) powered by generative AI in SAP Sales Cloud V2 can turn unstructured inputs—like emails or requests—into structured, actionable sales data. More importantly, you’ll see how this capability directly impacts real business outcomes: faster response times, improved quote quality, reduced manual effort, and a more scalable sales process. By the end, you’ll have a clear view of how to translate AI into measurable value and how to easily activate it for your sales operations—not as a concept, but as a practical, ready-to-use AI-CRM case scenario.

 

Process story and business flow

A quote request rarely arrives in a perfect format.

More often, it comes as an email with a mix of free text, item references, quantities, delivery expectations and so on. Sometimes there is also extra context hidden in the wording: this is for a shutdown window, this should follow an existing agreement, or this request is linked to a wider project.

That is usually where the work begins.

Before pricing, validation, or commercial alignment even starts, someone has to read the request, identify what matters, interpret the context, and turn all of that into something structured enough to create a sales quote. In more complex buying environments, this is not unusual - customers may use different interaction channels during their buying journey, which helps explain why inbound email quote requests often arrive fragmented across formats and touchpoints rather than as a single clean transaction.

A typical example could be this:

Please quote 24 units of valve kit VK-220 and 12 filter assemblies FA-90 for our Porto facility. We need delivery before the maintenance shutdown in week 39. Please align pricing with our existing agreement and see the attached sheet for the full list.

This is where Business Information Extraction (BIE) in SAP Sales Cloud Version 2 becomes relevant.

SAP documents BIE as the capability used to configure extraction logic for AI-assisted creation of sales quotes from emails, allowing organizations to define the scenario that interprets the incoming request and supports quote creation. 

So, in simple terms, the flow could look like this:

 

What changes here is not that the seller disappears from the process. What changes is that the seller can start from a better first draft instead of spending unnecessary time decoding the request.

Business benefits

The value of Business Information Extraction (BIE) goes beyond data extraction itself.
Its real impact lies in how it streamlines and improves the entire quote process, from request intake to final quote creation.

Each benefit shown below reflects a direct improvement in the flow once extraction is embedded early in the process:

 

 

Hence, BIE doesn’t just extract data  - it stabilizes, accelerates, and adapts the entire quote process end-to-end.

Use case scenarios and the flexibility of elements and classes

This is where BIE becomes more interesting.

SAP’s configuration model is not just about extracting static fields. It is built around three design components:

  • Elements, which represent explicit values to be extracted - I.e for quotes: Product ID, Product Name, Quantity and unit, request date and custom elements tailored to specific business needs, such as customer reference numbers.
  • Classes, which represent values derived from the content - Delivery Priority and custom classes tailored to specific business needs, such as customer type, request category, product family, and so on.
  • As a result of combining both elements and classes - Flexible scenarios can be defined, bringing these components together into a structured, business-relevant use case that adapts the extraction logic to specific processes and requirements.
     

That means the email-to-quote use case can be adapted depending on how the business actually receives requests.

Therefore, a simple scenario may focus on the elements and classes:

  • Element: Product ID
  • Element: Product Name
  • Element: Quantity
  • Element; Requested Date
  • Classes: Delivery Priority (Urgent request, Right away)

and that alone can already reduce a lot of manual effort!

But many organizations will want to go further. For example, a more operational quote flow could include elements such as:

  • Customer material number
  • contract reference
  • asset or serial reference

And it could include classes such as:

  • request type: new product, replacement
  • delivery priority: urgent, standard, planned
  • commercial context: contract-linked, project-driven, ad hoc
  • product family: spare part, accessory, consumable, finished good

This is where the “configurable to each customer’s specific business scenario” message really matters. The same BIE capability can be adapted to a customer that mostly handles recurring replenishment requests, and also to one that works with more project-heavy, location-specific, or contract-sensitive quote motions. Hence, SAP Sales Cloud V2 administrators configure the scenario used for AI-assisted sales quotes from emails, which is what makes this flexibility operationally meaningful rather than just conceptual.

Value accelerator path for easy activation

Understanding the use case is important, but making it easy to activate is what turns the idea into something real. The value accelerator path below brings together the main prerequisites, configuration steps, and scenario design choices needed to enable Business Information Extraction for AI-assisted email-to-quote.

See further about the prerequisites and configuration steps HERE.

​Addressing Business KPIs and Measuring outcomes early

Activating the use case is only part of the story. The next step is showing that it is improving the quote process in a visible and measurable way.

A practical starting point is to focus on four simple KPIs:

  • Time from request receipt to quote draft
    to show whether the intake process is becoming faster
  • Manual effort per quote
    to understand whether sellers spend less time on repetitive data capture
  • Rework caused by intake errors
    to track whether avoidable corrections are going down
  • First-pass quote completeness and tailored to your business need
    to see whether the first version of the quote is stronger, more complete and aligned to your business requirement

Together, these indicators help connect the use case to real operational outcomes, not just technical activation.

The best approach is to keep the first measurement cycle simple: start with one bounded scenario, define a baseline, measure the early impact, and refine from there. That kind of phased activation is usually the fastest way to make the use case real without turning it into a long design exercise.

 

Final thoughts

Business Information Extraction for email-to-quote helps bridge a common gap in the quote process: the gap between how customers send requests and how sales teams need that information to exist inside CRM.

That is what makes the use case compelling. It is not just an AI feature layered on top of a sales process. It is a practical mechanism to reduce friction where friction already exists, at the point where unstructured demand first enters the business.

And that is also why the configurability matters so much. The real strength is not only that BIE can extract data from unstructured emails, but that it can be shaped around the customer’s own quote scenario through the right combination of elements, classes, and scenario design. SAP’s current documentation supports setting up the model directly for AI-assisted sales quote creation from emails.

For teams working with SAP Sales Cloud V2, this makes BIE a very credible use case to activate: practical, business-relevant, configurable, and measurable. 

Thanks for reading!

entry.title: Re: Intercompany return process steps and scope item
entry.content:
In SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud, the intercompany return process is not fully symmetrical between issuing and receiving plants. Scope item 2F4 (Intercompany Returns) extends BKP (Customer Returns) but is designed around the receiving plant. For an issuing plant that wants to return goods, you cannot directly use the IC supplier; instead, you must model the process via a standard vendor return PO (NB/NB2) with a “normal” supplier, while intercompany logic is handled through the sales side of the partner company.

Why you can’t use IC Supplier directly is because, IC suppliers are tied to intercompany sales/stock transfer processes, not vendor returns. SAP’s design expects the receiving company to trigger the return (customer return), not the issuing company. Using IC supplier in a return PO would break the standard integration logic.

So the recommended approach is

  • Issuing Plant → Use NB/NB2 return PO with a normal vendor.
  • Receiving Plant → Use 2F4 scope item to process the return as customer returns.
  • Master Data Setup →
    • Maintain vendor master for the “normal supplier.”
    • Ensure customer master exists for the intercompany partner.
    • Map plants to company codes correctly for FI postings.

entry.title: Join Now: SAP Signavio Regional Challenge 2026
entry.content:
Exciting news for our community! We’re happy to share another opportunity for you to explore SAP technology in practice.

The SAP Signavio Regional Challenge 2026 invites students and academic participants across Latin America and the Caribbean to step into the world of business process transformation.

At its core, SAP Signavio is a leading solution that enables organizations to model, analyze and optimize business processes in a collaborative and data-driven way. Through this challenge, participants will gain hands-on experience with the same tools used by global companies to improve efficiency, identify bottlenecks, and drive innovation.

But this is more than just a learning experience. Throughout the journey, participants will engage in expert-led sessions, build practical knowledge and even work toward SAP Signavio certification.

The contest is open to students, professors, and academic participants connected to institutions within the SAP University Alliances ecosystem. No prior experience with SAP Signavio is required — the contest is designed to support learning along the way.

 

Key Event Details

The SAP Signavio Challenge is a regional, fully online initiative that invites students and academic participants across Latin America and the Caribbean to explore how businesses can improve the way they operate.

Once registered, participants gain access to SAP Signavio environment, onboarding materials and are guided through each stage of the journey: from understanding process thinking to applying it in practice. 

At the center of the experience is a business challenge experience, where participants will:

  • Analyze a business process
  • Identify inefficiencies and improvement opportunities
  • Design optimized solutions using SAP Signavio
  • Present their results in a structured and impactful way

This journey provides a clear understanding of how data, processes and technology come together to drive better business decisions and outcomes.

Recognition and Outcomes: Participants who complete the contest will receive an official certificate of participation, recognizing their engagement and achievement throughout the challenge.

Beyond certification, this experience delivers real, tangible value. Participants will:

  • Gain hands-on experience with SAP Signavio, a leading solution used by global companies
  • Develop practical skills in process analysis, problem-solving, and business optimization
  • Learn how to approach challenges with a consulting mindset, from identifying inefficiencies to proposing data-driven solutions
  • Build a stronger professional profile with concrete experience 

 

Agenda Overview

The challenge unfolds across key milestones where each step builds toward your final solution:

  • April 22 | Final day to register
    Registration closes and the challenge officially begins.
  • April 27 | Understanding what’s shaping tomorrow’s businesses
    Gain insights into AI trends and how SAP Signavio is applied to solve real organizational challenges.
  • April 28–29 |  Hands-on learning
    Participate in interactive workshops on BPMN 2.0 and SAP Signavio.
  • May 22 | Final submission
    Submit your optimized (TO-BE) process and simulation results.
  • May 29 | Final presentations and results
    Participants will present their solutions during the Winners Challenge, where the final results will be announced.

 

How to Participate

Joining is simple:

  1. Register through the official SAP Signavio LAC Contest page
  2. Confirm your participation via email
  3. Access onboarding materials and tools
  4. Start developing your solution

⏳ Important: Make sure to register before April 22 to secure your spot.

 

This is your opportunity to move beyond theory and experience how real business transformation happens.

Register now and be part of the SAP Signavio Regional Challenge 2026.

entry.content: I’m Vishwa Bharadhwaj, an engineering student trying to understand where I can actually create value with what I learn.I don’t have everything figured out yet. I know I don’t want a role where I just execute tasks without thinking. I want to work on problems that exist outside textbooks. Problems where the solution changes something real. Right now I’m drawn to the intersection of engineering and AI. I’ve seen how systems improve when data is used properly. I want to be part of building that. Not just studying it.I’ve noticed something about how I learn. When I see a concept applied in a real scenario, it sticks. That’s why areas like SAP, automation, and data started making more sense to me. I don’t enjoy learning in isolation. I prefer building, testing, and seeing outcomes.In the next few years, I want to become someone who can take a vague problem and turn it into a working solution. Not perfect. But useful. Something people can rely on.I’m still in the process of getting there.

entry.title: Re: SAP UI5 - Component-Based Architecture: Shell, Feature and Modules
entry.content:
Hey @wridgeu , good question!

To be honest I can only really speak for the frontend and architecture side here.
Backend design is handled by our backend developers and I don't want to claim things I can't fully back up.

What I can say is that our applications in the monorepo share some services and have dedicated ones for others. The services expose certain EntitySets and Actions for specific use cases, so I would not say they are fully generic. Maybe only in parts.

Whether the backend is truly decoupled from the concrete use case is probably a better question for the backend side. From a frontend perspective we just try to keep the input a module needs as minimal as possible, for example just a GUID, and let the module take care of the rest from there.

entry.title: AI with SAP
entry.content:
Core concept is as follows. In the age of AI, the most unstructured data/information is so well organised. But the most organised data in RDBMS and/or SAP systems is still opaque to business users who do not have expertise on SAP Functional and SAP Technical Side.

PrimeAISAP is a set of projects being worked to make business users TALK to SAP using natural language for all their informational/decisional needs on day to day basis.

This started as a need to one of our auto component manufacturing customer's CEO 10 years ago wanting to talk to SAP and get all answers back in natural language. We did some work on automating a layer on top of BO/SAP and text-to-speech and speech-to-text layer. The project was not successful for various reasons, but main reason is it lacked LLM like thinking. It also lacked ability to talk on variety of knowledge sources. This is what we are working on #iitb #akashmavle @AIwithSAP @aiandsap

entry.title: Missing Item Notes in Purchase Contract Output in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud
entry.content:
In this blog post, I would like to share an issue we encountered in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud where certain item texts were not appearing in the Purchase Contract output, even though they were maintained correctly. This may help others facing similar inconsistencies in text determination.

 

Issue:

In Purchase Contracts, the Info Record PO Text and Material PO Text were maintained and displayed in the document, but were missing in the generated output form.

After analyzing the affected items, we noticed that the missing texts were assigned the text status '*'. This status indicates that the text is available but not automatically adopted into the document output unless manually selected.

 

Root Cause:

The F1 help for the status pointed us to Customizing activity 104066 Define Copying Rules for Item Texts in Contracts. In our configuration, the copying rule for these text types was set to '*', meaning the text is displayed to the user, but must be manually adopted into the document if it should appear in the output.

 

Solution:

We adjusted the copying rule for the relevant text types from '*' to a blank value. After this change, the item texts were automatically adopted and appeared correctly in the Purchase Contract output without manual intervention.

 

Summary:

If you notice missing item notes in Purchase Contract output, it’s worth checking Customizing activity 104066 and verifying the copying rules for Material PO Text, Info Record PO Text, and any other relevant text types.

We were expecting an automatic adoption of the texts in Purchase Contract Output, however our customizing was set up for a manual adoption.

entry.title: Re: Validation of document item text does not work with a...
entry.content:
Hello @djwafelap

Given the explanation you provided on your client’s target, I would not recommend splitting the logic into “one rule for Create Supplier Invoice” and “one rule for all other apps” based on app or app ID precondition. The reason is because of the framework in the Journal Entry.

So, for your specific idea: Can we set a specific app or app ID as a precondition, and then work with two rules?

I would not recommend that. The framework field in Public Cloud is business context driven and not app-ID driven. That means what you are proposing is not reliable for your scenario. Considering your client wants to ensure that all final accounting lines carry a text, regardless of account type, then your original validation on DocumentItemText is conceptually actually the correct target field, because it checks the final accounting document item text, not just the UI entry field. See KBA 3066300 for more insights.

The lowest effort realistic design if your goal is one common enforcement rule, no maintenance of many field status groups and no dependency on posting key field status configuration, then I would recommend:

  • You keep one validation rule on DocumentItemText in the Journal Entry context
  • Add one substitution for the supplier invoice scenario to fill DocumentItemText for the lines that otherwise remain empty
  • Use header text or another business-defined source as the fallback text for those generated lines.

This will help you avoid trying to maintain 30 field status groups and avoids relying on posting key field status customisation.

On your question about posting key field status, in Public Cloud, you do not have the classic on premise posting key customisation pattern. That is why trying to solve this through posting key field status is not the direction I would suggest here. The cleaner Public Cloud approach is substitution plus validation.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Requirement – Credit Management for Cement Sales Orders
entry.content:
We have a requirement related to Credit Management in SAP S/4HANA RISE.

Requirement:
If a customer’s overdue is ≥ 90 days, the system should not allow creation of a Sales Order. This restriction should be applicable only for Cement Sales. For other materials, no change is required and the process should remain as is.

Current Business Scenario:

  • We are currently using credit management at the delivery level (Credit Group 02 – Delivery).
  • There is no restriction during Sales Order creation; all checks are applied at the time of delivery creation.
  • For oldest open items, a limit of 180 days is maintained, beyond which the system restricts processing.

New Requirement:
If the customer’s oldest open item is greater than 90 days, the system should not allow Sales Order creation specifically for Cement materials. For other materials (such as TMT, Wire Rod, etc.), the process should continue as per the current business.

Kindly suggest how this requirement can be fulfilled.
Also, please advise whether creating a separate order type for Cement Sales is required, as currently we are using the same order type for all domestic sales.

@Lakshmipathi Sir, please suggest & let me know if any additional info required from my end.

SD (Sales and Distribution) SD Billing 

Regards,

Soumya Nath.

entry.title: Re: Requirement – Credit Management for Cement Sales Orders
entry.content: You can fulfill this requirement in SAP S/4HANA RISE by enhancing credit management rules to trigger a credit check at Sales Order creation specifically for Cement materials. This can be achieved either by using material-specific credit check configurations (via risk category or product hierarchy) or by introducing a separate order type for Cement sales. A separate order type is not strictly mandatory, but it often simplifies configuration and reporting.

entry.title: Re: Requirement is to create multiple inspection lots for...
entry.content:
SAP standard does not create multiple inspection lots for the same PO item + batch combination. This is consistent across on-premise and public cloud.

The behavior you are seeing is actually standard QM integration. When you post multiple partial goods receipts against the same PO line and batch, the system updates the same inspection lot rather than creating new ones. This is by design and the inspection lot is created at the combination of PO item + batch + inspection type, so subsequent GRs for the same batch are merged.

Below are the possible approaches

1) Batch Differentiation
If you want separate inspection lots, you must post the subsequent GRs with a different batch number. Each batch triggers a new inspection lot.

2) Inspection Lot Creation Settings
Check QM customizing for inspection lot creation (Quality Management → Quality Inspection → Inspection Lot Creation). But in S/4HANA Public Cloud, customizing options are limited compared to on-premise.

3) BAdI Enhancement
In public cloud, you can explore available Business Add-Ins (BAdIs) for inspection lot creation. For example, BADI_QM_LOT_CREATE (on-premise) allows influencing lot creation logic. In S/4HC, check if equivalent released BAdIs exist in the Extensibility Catalog. You could add logic that if partial GR for same batch, force creation of new inspection lot.

4) Process Adjustment
If business requires separate lots for each partial GR, consider changing the process to always use unique batches per receipt. This aligns with SAP’s intended design.

entry.title: Re: SAP Build Apps Deprecation and The Path Forward
entry.content: @Venkat_Vyza thanks for your question. All other standalone offerings of SAP Build are still available as well as within the SAP Build offering as announced last year April. SAP Build Apps will be deprecated and is in maintenance mode. Please see the FAQs in the blog post which tools to use under which circumstances. The SAP Build Apps part within the SAP Build offering is only a fraction of the standalone offering - see here. Therefore, we highly recommend using the pro-code offering as of now for urgent projects. And also in the SAP Build offering there will be no additional SAP Build Apps features.

entry.title: Re: Help on material vs OA coverage report
entry.content: Could you please clarify whether you are referring to outline agreements on the purchase side (vendor contracts/scheduling agreements) or on the sales side (customer contracts)? The tcodes differ depending on the context. For example, on the purchase side you can use ME3M / ME3L to check materials covered by vendor agreements, while on the sales side you would use VA45 to list customer contracts. Once I know which side you mean, I can suggest the exact approach to check coverage for your material list.”

entry.title: Re: Requirement – Credit Management for Cement Sales Orders
entry.content: Sir, thank you for your response. Could you please guide me on how to trigger a credit check at the time of Sales Order creation specifically for Cement materials? Challenges Faced: 1. Single Customer for Multiple Products o Same customer is used for Cement as well as other products. o In SAP Transaction BP, the Check Rule is maintained at customer level, so it applies globally. o Hence, we are unable to differentiate credit behavior based on material using standard configuration. 2. No Check Rule Control in OVA8 (FSCM) o In S/4HANA FSCM, OVA8 does not contain Check Rule assignment, only:  Credit Control Area  Risk Category  Credit Group o Therefore, we cannot assign different check rules (e.g., 90 days vs 180 days) based on process or material via OVA8. 3. Material-Based Control Not Available in Standard o Standard FSCM logic is customer-centric, not material-specific. o We need product-specific credit control, which is not directly achievable via standard config. 4. Order Type Limitation o We are currently using a single order type for all domestic sales. o Even if we create a separate order type for Cement:  Users can still create orders with other order types and include Cement materials  This creates a control gap

entry.title: Re: SAP Public Cloud PR Workflow
entry.content:
1. Workflow Substitution
Use the My Inbox substitution feature. Another user can be nominated as a substitute approver for the requester. When the workflow step reaches the requester (who is excluded), the substitute will receive the task.

2. Workflow Configuration – Dynamic Agent Determination
In the workflow configuration, you can add logic to check if approver = requester → reassign to alternate approver (e.g., manager or predefined role). This can be achieved using responsibility rules or BAdI for agent determination (if released in Public Cloud). In S/4HANA Cloud, check the Business Workflow Extensibility Catalog for available BAdIs such as DetermineApprover for PR workflows.

3. Escalation / Error Handling
Configure escalation paths in the workflow. If the system encounters an error (approver = requester), it can escalate to the next level approver automatically. This avoids manual intervention.

entry.title: error sap note 3694434
entry.content:
Hello,

I am applying SAP Note 3694434 and ran into an error in function module J_1B_NF_IV_MATKL_DETERMINE. The note creates a line that uses the NF_TYPES structure, but this structure is not declared in the function.

I have already opened an incident with SAP, but they were not able to help me.

Has anyone who has already applied this note, or who has a more up-to-date system, been able to check where this structure is declared?

entry.title: Re: SAP Public Cloud PR Workflow
entry.content:
Dear @Ashley_Hung,

For Public Cloud if you configure Exclude Requestors and the PR requestor is also the only approver for that step, SAP states that if no recipients are determined after exclusion the workflow goes into Error unless the step is marked optional

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/0e602d466b99490187fcbb30d1dc897c/bacc21e14d6c4b0fbf53dca3218abdd5.html

In that situation the standard recovery is not to directly reassign the PR document itself. The standard admin action is to go to Flexible Workflow Administration and use Resume after Error / Resume Workflow after you have corrected the recipient determination so that a valid approver can now be found. SAP documents both the app capability and that resuming re-triggers the workflow from the failed point

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/a630d57fc5004c6383e7a81efee7a8bb/4b818514f0a2453bb72febf44b217f71.html

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/a630d57fc5004c6383e7a81efee7a8bb/5d505ae2259e409b8487a4dbaa3b6cc4.html

Summary:

SAP explicitly says you can forward tasks of one user to another user for tasks that are ready for processing so if your PR is already in Error because no approver could be determined, forwarding is usually not the fix because no valid approval task was created for a real approver in the first place

Also SAP supports:

  • marking the step as optional, so it is skipped if no approver is found and
  • using custom agent determination for PR workflow via Badi MMPUR_WORKFLOW_AGENTS_V2, which is the standard extensibility option to derive an alternative approver

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/a630d57fc5004c6383e7a81efee7a8bb/2966c6fc0b2c4667bd1511f974f187fe.html

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/a630d57fc5004c6383e7a81efee7a8bb/b2cd80f3d0e94efd806c3f535b5395fa.html

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/0e602d466b99490187fcbb30d1dc897c/31cc91282192493cbb651c132d966811.html

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/0e602d466b99490187fcbb30d1dc897c/720dcec59d264114bc4ec19759aa4514.html

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/0e602d466b99490187fcbb30d1dc897c/5ad74a497e8b4c8c8f1b10f705fe4e00.html

Best Regards,

Dawid

When creating a request manually, Accounting # is seems to be pulled in from the backend SAP systems but SP isn't connecting to those systems for attributes.

Has anyone experienced this and have a workaround?

Thank you.

entry.title: eSocial - Nota Técnica Nº 06/2026
entry.content:
Olá pessoal,  

Comunicamos a entrega das atualizações relacionadas à Nota Técnica Nº 06/2026 do eSocial S-1.3, que traz importantes modificações para garantir a conformidade legal. 

Principais Modificações 

Validação da Data de Comunicação de Óbito nos Eventos de Emprego 

O sistema agora valida algumas datas chave dos eventos de empregado contra a data de comunicação de óbito do empregado. 

Eventos afetados: 

  • S-2190 - Admissão de trabalhador - Registro preliminar 
  • S-2200 - Cadastramento Inicial do Vínculo e Admissão/Ingresso de Trabalhador 
  • S-2299 - Desligamento 
  • S-2300 - Trabalhador sem vínculo de emprego - início 
  • S-2399 - Trabalhador sem vínculo de emprego - término 

Quando qualquer data-chave desses eventos (como datas de admissão, transferência ou desligamento) for posterior à data de comunicação de óbito, o sistema gera mensagens de aviso para correção dos dados antes da submissão. 

Novo Campo infoPatPrec no Evento S-2500 

O evento S-2500 - Processo Trabalhista foi atualizado com o novo campo infoPatPrec, conforme especificado na NT 06/2026. 

SAP Notes Entregues 

As modificações estão disponíveis nas seguintes SAP Notes: 

  • 3726139 - eSocial - NT 06/2026 - Employee death date validations 
  • 3723879 - eSocial - NT 06/2026 - S-2500 - Transformation object for labor lawsuit 
  • 3724389 - Prerequisite objects for SAP Note 3723879 

Lembrando que todas as entregas referentes ao eSocial você encontrará na SAP Note 3700197 - eSocial - Overview - 2026. 

Gostou desse post?  

Dê um Like e compartilhe o conteúdo com seus colegas.  

Fiquem à vontade para deixar um feedback, comentário ou pergunta sobre este Blog post no espaço abaixo. E não esqueçam de seguir a tag HCM Payroll Brazil na SAP Community para ficarem ligados nas últimas notícias. 

Um abraço,  
Ramiro Manoel 
Time de HCM Brasil

entry.title: "3-Dot" Loading Hang in Manage Replication App within SFM: Solving HTTP Error 431
entry.content:
Hi SAP Sustainability Community, I hope you're all doing great.

Have you ever encountered a situation where the Manage Replications app in SAP Sustainability Footprint Management (SFM) stays stuck on the loading screen with three animated dots?

While this might look like a system outage, our technical investigation into recent cases has identified a common root cause that is actually browser-related: HTTP Error 431 - Request Header Fields Too Large.

The Technical Breakdown:
This is not a functional bug within the SFM application logic. Instead, it occurs when the browser's request headers—usually due to an accumulation of cookies, session tokens, or long-lived authentication data—become too "heavy" for the server to process. The server then rejects the metadata handshake, preventing the app from initializing.

How to Identify and Fix It If you see the infinite loading screen, follow these steps to confirm:

  1. Open your browser Developer Tools (F12).

  2. Go to the Console tab.

  3. Look for the error: Failed to load resource: the server responded with a status of 431 (Request Header Fields Too Large).

The Resolution:

To resolve this, you need to clear the session overhead:

  • Clear Cookies & Cache: Specifically for your SFM domain (e.g., *.footprint.sustainability.cloud.sap).

  • Use Incognito/Private Mode: This ensures a clean header state for the session.

We have published a dedicated Knowledge Base Article with the full technical breakdown and official steps. You can find more details here: Link.

By following these simple steps, you can ensure your master data replication flows remain active for your carbon footprint calculations and auditor reviews.

Has anyone else encountered this? Let’s discuss in the comments!

Tags: #SAPSustainability #SFM #ManageReplications #SustainabilityFootprintManagement

entry.title: Data retention for profiles - Explanation on the Created vs Last Updated rule
entry.content:
Hi experts,

we are configuring the data retention logic for the profiles in our CDP instance.

We have the use case where a boolean is present and used as the main condition for profile deletion.
If this boolean is set to true, the profile should be deleted after retention period of 2 days. For this we are using this boolean as condition (when set to true) and we set a custom time period of 2 days with Rule Activation set to "Last Updated". 

Here is where the confusion starts - in the documentation it is not well explained what different options of Created and Last Updated for rule activation mean. No examples really.
We are concerned that using the "Last Updated" as rule activation here means that as long as the profile gets updated with some data - the 2 days retention period would reset. Is this correct? 

More information and example would be highly appreciated.

Thanks,
Filip

entry.title: Re: Requirement – Credit Management for Cement Sales Orders
entry.content:
1. Product Hierarchy / Material Group → Risk Category Mapping
Assign Cement materials to a distinct product hierarchy or material group. Use a custom enhancement (BAdI for credit check) to derive a different risk category when Cement is present in the order. Risk category then drives stricter credit rules (90 days). Other materials retain the standard risk category (180 days). This requires extensibility, since standard FSCM does not evaluate material groups.

2. BAdI Enhancement (most precise)
In S/4HANA Cloud, check released BAdIs such as BADI_SD_CREDIT_CHECK or BADI_SD_CM (names vary by release). There add logic such that if sales order contains Cement material → apply stricter overdue rule (90 days). Else → apply standard rule (180 days). This allows you to keep a single order type and single customer, but still differentiate by material.

entry.title: Update SAP Market Communication
entry.content:
Market Process Management (MPM) with Application Process Engine (APE): Moving Into the Core of SAP S/4HANA Utilities 2025

For several years now, Market Process Management (MPM) together with the Application Process Engine (APE) has been successfully supporting customers in the German and other deregulated energy markets. Introduced to the market in 2023, the solution has proven its robustness, scalability, and ability to handle complex market communication processes with high automation and transparency.

Due to this strong adoption and positive market feedback, SAP has decided to technically integrate MPM with APE directly into SAP S/4HANA Utilities 2025. This means:

  • MPM with APE will become part of the SAP S/4HANA Utilities core
  • No additional add‑on installation will be required moving forward

This strategic step ensures a streamlined architecture, simplifies lifecycle management, and strengthens MPM as the central framework for market communication processes in utilities.

Successor of IDEXGC

With its modern, enhanced capabilities, MPM with APE is the designated successor to IDEXGC.
As part of the shift to SAP S/4HANA Utilities 2025:

  • IDXGC 802 will not be released for SAP S/4HANA 2025–based systems
  • IDXGC will not be installable on S/4HANA 2025
    → This is an important consideration for all customers planning their transformation to SAP S/4HANA.

MPM with APE provides a future‑ready, scalable alternative, fully aligned with the long‑term roadmap of SAP Utilities.

Support for Customers Transitioning from IDXGC

SAP Services actively supports existing IDXGC customers on their transition journey to MPM (APE).
This includes:

  • Functional and technical guidance
  • Migration support
  • Best practices based on real‑world customer experiences

This ensures that utilities can make the shift smoothly while safeguarding continuity in market communication processes.

Additional Information

For further details and clarifications, please refer to the SAP Note:

🔗 SAP Note 1745581 — Additional Information on MPM & APE
https://me.sap.com/notes/1745581/E

entry.title: Re: Contribution Margin Forecast with SAP Business Data Cloud
entry.content:
Thanks for your interest, Michael - much appreciated.

Let me please answer as follows:

  1. Seamless Planning in BDC enhances the flexibility to decide where to perform calculations according to the use case. E.g., need for persistency such as budget break-downs would still happen via data actions while pure reporting measures (e.g., % of calculations) could be modeled in the Analytic Model. Finally, AI capabilities could be handled in Datasphere (HANA PAL) or via partner solutions (e.g., Databricks via BDC Connect).
  2. Real-time planning is indeed possible but the question is how close to "real-time" you need to get. Theoretically, you can combine purely federated (and in that sense real-time) data with SAC planning data. However, performance aspects in the frontend will determine if you need to have an intermediate snapshot/persistency, which can be close to real-time (steered via replication frequency).
  3. As pointed out under point 1, for specific use cases Data Actions will absolutely remain the best or even only possible solution, thus delays-to-insight driven by SAC Data Action batch processing can still occur. I would focus on performance-optimized data action design so that these could be triggered by end users ad-hoc rather than waiting for batch processes over night or similar.

I hope that clarifies your questions.

Cheers

You mentioned using Product Hierarchy / Material Group to derive a different Risk Category. o Could you please clarify how and where this mapping is done? o Is this achievable via standard configuration, or does it require a BAdI implementation? 2. Regarding the BAdI approach: o Which exact BAdI should be used (e.g., UKM_CHECK_STEP or any other)? o At what point in the process (Sales Order / Delivery) should the logic be implemented? o How can we dynamically apply different overdue thresholds (90 days for Cement, 180 days for others) within the same customer? 3. Since we are using a single customer and single order type for multiple materials, how can the system differentiate the credit check behavior at material level? 4. Also, is it recommended to: o Use only BAdI-based logic, or o Combine it with a separate order type for Cement for better control? If possible, I would really appreciate if you could provide a step-by-step approach or high-level design, including: • Configuration steps (if any) • Enhancement points • Best practice followed in similar scenarios This will help us implement the solution correctly. Thank you again for your support.

entry.title: SAP MDG News: AI Use Cases delivered, demoed and presented
entry.content:
Last week, I had the opportunity to present the latest SAP MDG and AI developments to a major client. Since the 2023 release, SAP MDG has unlocked numerous AI use cases, with most integrated directly into Joule and additional embedded AI features available. The live demo showcased all these capabilities in action.

Key AI Use Cases Overview

We're currently seeing three interaction types of Joule with MDG that are transforming how users work with master data:

Use Case 1: Informational

Knowledge at Your Fingertips: With Joule integrated into the SAP Launchpad, business users can ask MDG-related questions without leaving their current workspace. Instead of opening external browsers or applications, users can simply ask questions like "Does MDG support parallel processes for the same object?" Joule accesses the complete SAP Help documentation and SAP Community to deliver high-quality answers instantly.

Use Case 2: Navigational

Smart Data Discovery: Users can pose specific questions about master data within their MDG system. For example, asking "Show me all Business Partners with NWT in their name" displays results directly within the Joule chatbot. Users can then navigate directly from Joule to the appropriate app or action, eliminating the traditional process of opening the correct app, searching for business partners, and then proceeding with tasks.

 

Use Case 3: Transactional

Guided Process Creation: This powerful capability is already available in MDG cloud ready mode. When creating a new Business Partner via Joule, users interact within the chatbot interface, receiving guided assistance with the right questions for BP creation. The system also provides duplicate detection alerts directly within the chat experience

Custom AI Solutions Discussion

Beyond standard use cases, we explored customer-specific implementations using SAP Business AI to enhance master data quality. The concept of an "Attribute Recommendation Agent" could automatically improve data quality through regular evaluation and remediation processes, keeping master data current and accurate through automated quality checks.

Client Feedback Section

The feedback on delivered use cases was very positive! We also discussed exciting possibilities for implementing custom AI use cases with SAP Business AI to further enhance their master data governance processes.

Call to Action

For more detailed insights, check out this comprehensive blog: https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/meet-joule-your-ai-copilot-for-sap-master-data-governance/ba-p/14273811

Meet Joule - Your AI Copilot for SAP Master Data Governance 🤖

Best regards,

Steffen

entry.title: SAP Community Voice: SAP Champions and Mentor tips, new ABAP Keyword and upcoming SAP Inside Tracks!
entry.content:
Featured Blog Posts 

Fiori Elements Navigation Patterns 

SAP Champion Mio Yasutake published a valuable blog post last week, bringing light to Fiori Elements Navigation Patterns Showcase. That are multiple ways to navigate Fiori Elements from one application to another, While the SAPUI5 documentation explains each annotation in detail, it is often focused on code snippets. Which can be difficult sometimes to visualize how each navigation pattern behaves in the UI. To address this, Mio created a repository that demonstrates many Fiori Elements navigation patterns along with their actual UI behavior. On her blog post, you will find the link for the sample inside Github and her detailed explanation on how navigation is initiated and how data is passed to the target app. The repository allows developers to explore real examples, compare patterns like semantic links and intent-based navigation, and follow a simple decision guide to choose the right approach for their scenario. 

Read Mio Yasutake Blog post here 

Essential health checks for SAP Build Work Zone, standard edition and Joule 

SAP UX specialist Leslie Castle shares in his latest blog post a set of essential health checks for SAP Build Work Zone, standard edition and Joule, helping ensure your setup is fully functional before onboarding business users. The guide walks through key areas such as architecture validation, principal propagation, content exposure, identity provisioning, and troubleshooting tools, offering step-by-step checks to confirm everything is correctly configured. Performing these checks will allow you to become more confident and leverage the significant benefits of Joule. With real examples and clear testing scenarios, the post helps teams quickly identify and resolve issues, so they can confidently roll out Joule and start leveraging its full business value from day one. 

Read Leslie Castle Blog post here 

OData MCP Proxy 

SAP Mentor Wouter Lemaire, introduces the OData MCP Proxy: an open-source, configuration-driven server that exposes any OData V2 or REST API as MCP (Model Context Protocol) tools, making them instantly accessible to AI assistants like Claude, Cursor, or any MCP-compatible client. In his blog post, Wouter how this proxy runs seamlessly on SAP BTP, leveraging services like Destination Service and XSUAA for secure, scalable integration. He shows in detail the architecture, the whole configuration file structure, transport modes, and how AI agents can intelligently interact with SAP services. In the end of his post, you will find a list five MCP servers he built using this exact pattern, for a different SAP service each. We highly recommend looking into all his blog posts in this series, plus the links he shared for GitHub! 

Read Wouter Lemaire Blog post here 

Brand-new ABAP Keyword 

The last blog post highlight was written by SAP expert Kilian Kilger announcing a brand-new top level ABAP keyword in ABAP CE 2602. Designed to fully leverage the power of SAP HANA, the new “MERGE” command combines multiple database operations, such as INSERT, UPDATE, and DELETE-into a single, efficient statement. MERGE is a standard SQL database command. It exists on the HANA database and most other commercial databases. The post explains not only how “MERGE” works conceptually and through practical examples, but also the challenges of introducing new keywords in ABAP, including compatibility with existing macros. Due to its ability to join an internal or database table (or a global temporary table for that matter), it can be a very powerful tool in the hands of an ABAP developer.  You may find additional information in the SAP help article here.  

Read Kilian Kilger Blog post here 

Community News 

Ask me Anything with SAP Champions: Session 2 

In the past editions of the Community Voice, we have mentioned the amazing initiative from the SAP Champions program, the Ask me Anything series, and we are happy to let everyone know that the submission of questions for Session 2 is open! SAP Champion Marietta Lopes is leading the March edition, with more than 20 years of experience in SAP transformation programs, SAP S/4HANA implementations, enterprise architecture, governance frameworks, and digital innovation within the SAP ecosystem. This can be a great opportunity to get amazing advice from her on e.g. Clean Core in SAP Cloud ERP (Private & Public), RISE or GROW with SAP, Clean Core Governance & Methodologies and much more. To participate, you can either drop your question on the comments section of her blog post linked below or ask anonymously in the link here. 

Ask me Anything with SAP Champion Marietta 

Hello SAP Community: Blog Series in Beginners Corner 

March also marks the beginning of our new blog post series, the Hello SAP Community! Launched by the SAP Community Advocate Moshe Naveh, the initiative is a way of bringing light to the next generation of SAP professionals, creating a space where they can share more about them, what they have been studying, topics of interest, their vision of the future, how they grow in the era of AI and much more. The initiative has already started inside the Beginners Corner Group at SAP Community, with students from the Bangalore area. Every week the participants will post introduction blog posts about themselves that will allow us to get to know them, learn from them and connect. They will answer preset questions, and every participant will also add their own unique touch, insights and experiences.  Are you interested in participating? Make sure to check Moshe post, to find all the useful links, the preset questions and his contact for more information. 

Hello Community Blog Series  

Hobbies Series: Teaching in a School 

This week's Hobbies Series highlight is by the SAP Community member and engineering student, Abhijna blog post. She shares the story of how she started teaching and learning alongside young students, inspired by her grandfather’s impact as an educator. Two years ago she returned to the school where he was a teacher, with a desire to give back. What started with small interactions, like teaching dance and introducing basic concepts of engineering, quickly turned into meaningful moments of curiosity and connection. Through examples and simple explanations, she discovered that even complex topics can spark understanding when approached the right way. Her experience is a reminder that sometimes, creating impact isn’t about doing something big, but about showing up, sharing knowledge, and becoming a small reason for someone to dream bigger. We highly recommend checking her full story, and maybe you also feel inspired to spread your knowledge with more people around you 

Hobbies Series Abhijna Blog post here 

Events 

March Meet-up Recaps 

March was an amazing month filled with community-driven events all over the world. We would like to highlight some of the great recaps of location group members have shared:  

Starting with SAP Inside Track Joinville in Brazil, which took place on March 14 and was completely full of attendees! With a great recap shared by event organizer Gislaine, the event featured more than 7 sponsors and proved to be a big success. In case you also participated in the event, make sure to share your pictures and feedback in the comment section of Gislaine post or publish your own blog post in the Joinville group. 

Read SIT Joinville recap here 

Jumping to Casablanca, local group organizer Younes Elmir shared highlights from The Joule Effect event held last week at the SAP Casablanca office. Focused on AI topics, Younes described that what stood out most was the exchange between experienced professionals and emerging SAP talent. If you also would like to participate on the next events, you can join the Casablanca group and keep up with the updates. 

Read Casablanca event recap here 

Pune Stammtisch also took place last week, bringing local SAP community members together to discuss topics such as AI in the SAP space, Future of Intelligent ERP and much more. Event organizer Parineeta Somvanshi shared more details about the sessions in his latest recap, but for him the most special aspect was the great conversations that continued between sessions, people exchanging ideas and staying engaged throughout the event. If you are interested on the next upcoming events, or would like to share your own recap impressions, make sure to join the Pune group.  

Read Pune event recap here 

SAP Inside Track Sydney: Save the date 

Talking about opportunities to meet-up, we are happy with the announcement that the SAP Inside Track Sydney dates is finally out! On Friday, September 4, 2026, the event will happen at a new venue, for an elevated experience but with the same community spirit. SAP Champion Mustafa Bensam published the Save the Date on the events calendar page and soon he will publish more details including registration, call for speakers, and sponsorship opportunities. Stay tuned and don´t miss out in the opportunity to meet other Sydney group members, share knowledge and connect. 

SIT Sydney event post here 

SAP Inside Track Recife: Call out for Speakers 

Another great upcoming SIT is the SAP Inside Track Recife, that will happen on May 23 at the Hotel Manibu, in Boa Viagem. Since its first edition in 2026, this year the local group celebrates 10 years of SIT Recife and establishing itself as more than just a tech event. It’s a movement centered around people, experiences, and the continuous growth of the SAP ecosystem in Brazil. The registration for tickets and the agenda will soon to be announced.  But we would like to remind everyone that the Call for Speakers is open until April 10, and you just need to submit your proposal including topic, title and a short abstract to the email: recife@sitbrazil.com. Beyond the event announcement, SAP Champion Marssel Vilaca also have published blog posts with more details on how to apply for speaking and how to become a sponsor.  Check out each one of the posts inside Recife group to find out more details on how to support. 

SIT Recife event post here

entry.title: Re: Characters limit issue on the Comments section for Em...
entry.content: Hi John If you are using legacy PM, please refer to below KBA and try to add in the required field XML. 2483780 - Required Field Validation does not trigger during collaborative steps - Performance Management https://me.sap.com/notes/2483780/E If you are using latest PM, currently it is not supported to use above code. Reference KBA: https://me.sap.com/notes/3731234/E\n 3731234 - Required field validation not working using Save and Close (save_and_close) button - Latest Experience of Performance Management Forms If you have further questions, please feel free to create case to us. Best regards Marisa SAP Support
